1: The oath at Mizpah: 'None of us shall give his daughter in marriage to Benjamin' (lōʾ-yittēn ʾîš mibbanôtāyw ləḇinyāmîn ləʾiššāh). The oath was sworn during the original assembly at Mizpah before the battles, presumably as a punitive measure to isolate Benjamin socially after the war. Marriage alliances were the primary mechanism of political and social integration between Israelite tribal units — cutting Benjamin off from marriage connections meant cutting them off from the covenant social network. The oath, once sworn, was binding under ANE covenant law: breaking it incurred divine sanction.
2: The people come to Bethel, sit there before God until evening, and lift up their voices and weep bitterly — the formal lamentation posture for communal grief (cf. Judges 20:23,26: the same actions before the second and third battles). ʾădōnāy YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl (Lord YHWH, God of Israel) — the formal appellative used in solemn address to the covenant deity. The assembled community grieves the consequence of their own military success: they have nearly destroyed a tribe of Israel, and the oath they swore has made that destruction permanent.
3: The lament: 'Why, O YHWH, God of Israel, has this happened in Israel, that today one tribe should be lacking from Israel?' (lāmmāh YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl hāytāh zōʾt bəyiśrāʾēl). The theological assumption behind the lament is that the twelve-tribe structure of Israel belongs to YHWH's covenant promises — no tribe can simply be eliminated without disrupting the covenant order. The number twelve has symbolic-theological weight: twelve sons of Jacob, twelve tribes, twelve months, twelve stones (Joshua's memorial). The loss of a tribe is not merely demographic but covenantally destabilizing.
4: The assembly builds an altar and offers burnt offerings and peace offerings (wayyibənû šām mizbēaḥ wayyaʿălû ʿōlôt ûšəlāmîm) — the same combination as Judges 20:26, the penitential sacrifice before the victorious battle. The building of a new altar at Bethel alongside the existing sanctuary may reflect the extraordinary circumstances: the national assembly constructing its own altar in the crisis. The sacrifices express both consecration (burnt offerings) and covenant fellowship (peace offerings) — the community is seeking renewed YHWH-relationship in the aftermath of the catastrophe they helped create.
5: The second oath: 'What one of the tribes of Israel did not come up to YHWH at Mizpah?' — this oath called for death against any tribe that failed to participate in the muster. The demand for universal tribal participation in national judicial-military actions was based on covenant solidarity obligations. The application of the death penalty to non-participants reflects the amphictyonic principle: refusal to stand with the covenant community when called was itself a covenant violation (cf. the curse against Meroz in 5:23 for not helping YHWH against Sisera). The oath creates a second instrument for Israel's crisis.
6: Israel grieved for Benjamin their brother (wayyinnāḥămû bənê yiśrāʾēl ʾel-binyāmîn ʾāḥîw) — the term ʾāḥ (H251, brother) is the covenant-kinship term. nāḥam (H5162, to be sorry/repent/grieve — the same root as Noah's naming in Genesis 5:29 and YHWH's 'repenting' over human evil in Genesis 6:6) — the community experiences genuine grief over what their punitive action has produced. The grief is for a fellow Israelite, expressed in the language of brotherly solidarity that had been absent during the war. The emotional reversal — from military zeal to brotherly grief — sets up the complex moral situation of Judges 21.
7: The oath problem: 'What shall we do for wives for those who are left, since we have sworn by YHWH that we will not give them any of our daughters as wives?' The oath has created a dilemma: they want to preserve Benjamin, but their binding oath prevents them from giving daughters directly. ANE legal traditions recognized that binding oaths created obligations that could not simply be set aside, but that creative solutions might satisfy the letter of the oath while achieving the desired result. The chapter explores two such solutions: both are ethically troubling, suggesting that the narrator views the entire episode as a consequence of rash oath-swearing.
8: The investigation of Jabesh-Gilead's absence from the Mizpah muster — the city is identified with Tell Abu Kharaz in the Jabbok Valley, Transjordan (alternative: Tell el-Maqlub). Jabesh-Gilead will appear again prominently in connection with Saul (1 Sam 11: Saul rescues Jabesh from Nahash; Judges 31:11-13: Jabesh-Gilead's men retrieve Saul's body). The connection between Benjamin and Jabesh-Gilead established here is the basis for Jabesh's loyalty to Saul's house throughout the monarchy period. The absence of Jabesh from the muster is verified by census: ûpāqad hāʿām (H6485, mustering/numbering/inspecting — the same verb as 'visiting' and 'appointing').
9: The census confirms: no one from Jabesh-Gilead was present (wəhinnēh ʾên-šām mêyōšēḇ giḇʿāh — 'and behold, there was no one from the inhabitants of Jabesh-gilead'). The census at Mizpah was a tribal muster-roll: each tribal unit was accounted for, enabling identification of absent communities. The absence of Jabesh-Gilead provides the assembly with both a crisis solution (virgin girls of Jabesh as potential wives for Benjamin) and a punitive justification (the oath of death against non-participants).
10: Israel sends 12,000 warriors to Jabesh-Gilead with explicit instructions: kill every male, every woman who has known a man, but save virgin women. The force of 12,000 represents a significant detachment — 3% of the original 400,000 — suggesting a serious military operation rather than a punitive raid. The military commission against a fellow Israelite town applies the ḥērem formula (kill all non-virgins) that was just applied to Benjamin's cities. The logical and moral consistency of the assembly is coherent within its own framework but deeply troubling: one group nearly destroyed; another destroyed to save the first.
11: The specific instruction: destroy every male and every woman who has known a man (ʾeḵ hāʾiššāh yāḏəʿāh miškaḇ zākār taḥărîmû). The sexual-knowledge criterion for execution follows the same logic as the original crime investigation: sexual purity of women as a criterion for sparing or killing. The legal basis for distinguishing virgins from non-virgins in this military-demographic context (as wives for Benjamin) has no explicit Torah parallel — the assembly is improvising a solution from available legal-cultural resources. The women of Jabesh-Gilead become pawns in a crisis created by the men's rash oaths.
12: 400 virgin girls were found among the inhabitants of Jabesh-Gilead. The population mathematics: Benjamin had 600 survivors (Judges 20:47); 400 Jabesh women falls 200 short. The deficiency sets up the Shiloh episode (vv19-22). The women are brought to the camp at Shiloh in the land of Canaan — Shiloh (Khirbet Seilun, 35 km north of Jerusalem) was the location of the tabernacle/ark in this period (Joshua 18:1; 1 Sam 1-4). The assembly at Shiloh marks the transfer from the war's Mizpah-Bethel axis to the central sanctuary.
13: Israel sends a peace offer to Benjamin at the rock of Rimmon (šālom H7965 — the formal offer of peace ending hostilities, the ANE counterpart to a treaty or armistice). The 600 Benjaminites at Rimmon have been in the wilderness for four months (Judges 20:47). The peace offer presumably includes assurances of safety and the proposed solution to the wife-shortage. Rimmon to Shiloh involves a journey of approximately 20 km — Benjamin coming out of their wilderness refuge to the central sanctuary marks the formal end of the civil war and the beginning of restoration.
14: Benjamin returns, and the 400 Jabesh-Gilead women are given to them. The 200 remaining without wives means the solution is still incomplete — prompting the Shiloh plan. The giving of women as war prizes/captives was standard ANE military practice: Deuteronomy 21:10-14 regulates the marriage of captured women, requiring a month's mourning period before marriage. The narrative does not comment on whether this requirement was followed, but the provision exists in Torah. The women of Jabesh-Gilead are technically captives of the Israelite army, given to Benjamin as wives — a second-generation application of the Deuteronomic captive-wife provision.
15: The people had compassion on Benjamin (wayyinnāḥămû hāʿām ləḇinyāmîn) — the same root as v6 (nāḥam), expressing grief-generated desire to restore. pereṣ (H6556, breach/rupture/gap — the word used for Achan's sin creating a 'breach' in Numbers 25:9's Perez-Uzzah) — YHWH has made a breach in the tribes. The attribution of the breach to YHWH (ʾăšer-ʿāśāh YHWH pereṣ bəšiḇṭê yiśrāʾēl) is the theological interpretation: the civil war's destruction of Benjamin was YHWH's judgment on the tribe, not merely Israel's military success. YHWH's agency in the breach is the premise for seeking YHWH's restoration.
16: The elders' problem: how shall we provide wives for those remaining since the women of Benjamin have been destroyed? The Benjaminite women were all killed in the war (Judges 20:48: 'all the cities they came to, they put to the sword'). The 400 Jabesh women filled some slots, but 200 men remain without wives. The elders are the deliberative body responsible for the solution — the same tribal-elder structure that failed to enforce covenant justice in the first place must now exercise creative problem-solving to reverse the demographic catastrophe that their collective decisions produced.
17: The elders' declaration: 'There must be an inheritance for the survivors of Benjamin' (yərūšat pəlêṭāh ləḇinyāmîn). yərūšāh (H3425, inheritance/possession — the covenant term for tribal territorial allotment, Joshua 12:6-7) — the tribal inheritance cannot continue without descendants. pəlêṭāh (H6413, remnant/those who escape/survived) — the remnant theology: even after judgment, YHWH preserves a remnant to continue the covenant line. The elders' legal and theological reasoning is correct: the dissolution of a tribe would violate the covenant structure. The problem is that their two oaths (no daughters; death to non-participants) block the obvious solution.
18: The first oath's binding force: 'We cannot give them wives from our daughters' because of the sworn oath (ʾănaḥnû lōʾ-nûkal lātēt lāhem nāšîm mibbanôtênû kî-ḥilû bənê-yiśrāʾēl lēʾmōr). ANE oath law (including biblical law) treated sworn oaths as binding even if their consequences were severe (cf. Jephthah's daughter in Judges 11). The impossibility of directly giving daughters is what forces the assembly to seek the indirect solution of the Shiloh festival. The narrative implicitly criticizes the rash oath-swearing: the same people who swore this oath now must engineer a legal workaround to avoid the consequences.
19: The Shiloh solution: an annual feast of YHWH at Shiloh (ḥag YHWH bəšilōh miyyāmîm yāmîmāh — 'a feast of YHWH at Shiloh from year to year'). The annual feast (ḥag H2282 — a pilgrimage festival involving dancing and celebration) at Shiloh is attested in 1 Samuel 1:3 (Elkanah's annual pilgrimage) and 1 Samuel 1:21. The festival involved dancing in the vineyards — a harvest celebration combining religious observance with communal festivity. North of Bethel, east of the highway, south of Lebonah — the geographical triangulation locates Shiloh precisely for travelers coming from multiple directions.
20: The instruction to the 200 Benjaminites: lie in wait in the vineyards and watch (ʾirəbû bakkaramîm). ʾāraḇ (H693, to lie in wait/ambush) — the same word used for the ambush at Gibeah (Judges 20:29,33) and the military ambush at Ai (Joshua 8:4). The application of ambush vocabulary to wife-seizure is jarring: it frames the capture of women at a religious festival using the same military-tactical language as the civil war. The narrative does not endorse the action — it reports it. The women who are 'seized' are described as the daughters of Shiloh dancing in the festival, not as war captives.
21: When the daughters of Shiloh come out to dance, Benjamin should each seize a wife for himself from the daughters of Shiloh and go to the land of Benjamin. The festival dance in the vineyards was likely a harvest celebration associated with the grape harvest (cf. Isaiah 16:10; Jeremiah 48:33 for vineyard celebrations). The dancing was public and participated in by unmarried young women. That Benjamin is instructed to 'seize' (waḥăṭaptem H2414, to snatch/seize) — not court, negotiate, or pay bride-price — reflects the extremity of the situation: the normal marriage protocols (negotiation, bride-price, parental consent) are being deliberately bypassed to avoid triggering the oath.
22: The anticipated complaint management: if their fathers or brothers come to complain, say: 'Grant them graciously to us, because we did not take a wife for each man in battle; for you did not give them to us; otherwise you would now be guilty'. The legal argument is tortured: the fathers did not 'give' the daughters (which would violate the oath), so they are not oath-breakers; the daughters were 'taken' without the fathers' explicit consent. The argument's weakness is its own evidence that the assembly knows this is ethically problematic. The instruction to manage complaints rather than prevent them suggests awareness that the scheme is morally borderline.
23: Benjamin does as instructed: each took his wife from the dancers, and they went and returned to their inheritance (wayyišəbû ʾel-naḥălātām). The return to their tribal naḥălāh (inheritance/allotment) is the covenantal goal: Benjamin as a tribe with territory and descendants can perpetuate itself. Rebuilt the cities and settled there (wayyibənû ʾet-hāʿārîm wayyēšəbû bāhem) — Benjamin begins reconstructing their devastated tribal infrastructure. The rebuilding of Benjaminite cities is the first constructive act since the war began in Judges 19; it signals the resumption of covenant normalcy even in the aftermath of catastrophe.
24: Israel departs, every man to his tribe and to his clan (ʾîš ləšiḇṭô ûləmišpaḥtô) — the dispersal formula for the end of a national assembly. The tribal-clan structure reasserts itself after the exceptional national gathering. The departure from Shiloh back to tribal territories is the final act of the Judges narrative before its closing verdict. The twelve-tribe structure is nominally intact: Benjamin, even devastated and rebuilt with seized wives, remains a tribe with territory and heirs. The covenant structure survives, barely — but what has been preserved is a severely damaged version of what YHWH's covenant community was meant to be.
25: The closing verdict: 'In those days there was no king in Israel. Everyone did what was right in his own eyes' (bayyāmîm hāhēm ʾên meleḵ bəyiśrāʾēl ʾîš hayyāšār bəʿênāyw yaʿăśeh). The refrain (Judges 17:6; Judges 18:1; Judges 19:1; Judges 21:25) reaches its final and most resonant occurrence here, after the book's darkest episodes: private idolatry (Micah's shrine), tribal theft (Dan's theft of Micah's priest), sexual violence and murder (Gibeah), civil war (Judges 20-21), and the capture of women at a religious festival. The final verdict interprets all of it through the lens of kinglessness: without a human king enforcing the Deuteronomic covenant, every person (and tribe) becomes their own moral authority — with catastrophic results. The book ends as a long argument for the monarchy, specifically for a king after YHWH's own heart.
